jerome-masi.heroHook: French illustrator in Annecy: bold flat color, minimal figures, childhood & silence—digital for global brands, acrylic for personal canvases.
jerome-masi.storyFullText:
Jérôme Masi is a freelance art director and illustrator in Annecy, represented by Creasenso—École Emile-Cohl in Lyon, then video games, a Lyon graphic studio founded in 2006, collective years, motion design, and finally full-time illustration.

Commercial work stays largely digital for brands such as Orange, Tissot, and British Airways; personal pieces return to acrylic on canvas between desk and easel.

He describes his own taste as sober and minimal; the cover work “Childhood” embodies silence, interiority, and childhood, and he wants viewers to fill the intentional gaps in the image themselves.

On a Street Lamp, his flat color, strong silhouettes, and restrained palettes stay clear—graphic work that keeps its identity when lit.

moritz-adam-schmitt.heroHook: Cologne illustrator: flat, saturated vector scenes; broke out via “One Icon a Day,” Adobe MAX 2016, and Adobe Stock—bold work built for screens and products.
moritz-adam-schmitt.storyFullText:
Moritz Adam Schmitt is a Cologne illustrator who broke in through a year of “One Icon a Day” on Instagram, Adobe MAX 2016, and Adobe’s 2017 “New Face” spotlight—freelance from 2016 on.

He stacks research, reference, iPad roughs in Photoshop Sketch, then vector inside Adobe Creative Cloud.

Street attitude, pop palettes, and icon-ready flat shapes define the commercial lane.

High-contrast flat illustration reads fast on a Street Lamp, so editions stay legible as something you live with—not only on a screen.

loreta-isac.heroHook: Bucharest illustrator & animator: dreamy figurative work—digital and paint—framed as “frozen moments” for editorial and global brands.
loreta-isac.storyFullText:
Loreta Isac is a Bucharest illustrator and animator who still opens her arc as “once upon a dreamer,” rooted in Iași before the capital.

Illustration, animation, and motion share one desk; Agent Pekka and COLOR studio Bucharest back the practice alongside clients like Apple, The New York Times, and The Washington Post.

She describes her journal-based practice as collecting “frozen moments” and draws from people, nature, events, and hard-to-name emotions.

Figurative, narrative scenes stay emotionally readable on a Street Lamp—story and detail hold up at edition scale.

dawal.heroHook: Paris-based painter and muralist (@_dawal): press profiles describe surreal mini-frescoes on cracked walls and studio work; Creapills and A Day Magazine covered the practice.
dawal.storyFullText:
Dawal is the Paris-associated painter and street artist building surreal narratives straight on the wall—often into cracked plaster, turning damage into micro-compositions.

The work treats city surfaces as imagination prompts; photo trails and indexes round out the map.

Instagram lives at @_dawal; a pinned post nods to a Paris solo threaded with childhood motifs.
dawal.additionalHistoryText: Web research (verify): Self-taught French street artist based in Paris; born 1994. Began painting in streets around 18–19, influenced by hip-hop, moving from lettering toward character-driven work (Posca interview). Open studio at Shakirail, Paris 18e, since Oct 2023. Practice includes murals, canvas, drawing, sculpture; transforms wall imperfections into miniature dreamlike scenes (Creapills and other press).

emelio-cerezo.activeSince: Graffiti since 2001; fine arts University of Granada 2004–2009; postgraduate drawing 2009–2010 (UrbanPresents interview)
emelio-cerezo.heroHook: Spanish painter Emilio Cerezo (@emiliocerezo): UrbanPresents traces graffiti aliases 2001–2012, fine-arts training in Granada, and mural work linked to the 626 crew.
emelio-cerezo.storyFullText:
Emilio Cerezo is a Totana-born painter and muralist who traded early graffiti tags for his given name around 2012.

Granada (2004–2009), postgraduate drawing, and media-industry years in Murcia and Granada shaped the pivot.

New walls keep appearing across Spain and beyond—coordinates accumulate street by street.
emelio-cerezo.additionalHistoryText:
Auto-extracted from primary source page (verify before publishing):

When Emilio turned 18 years old, he moved to Granada to study fine arts at the University of Granada from 2004 until 2009 and did a postgrad in drawing at the same place from 2009 to 2010. At the same time he learned about classical painting at the University, and it made him grow as a painter. Looking back now, he can see some connections between his current works and some content he studied long time ago. His images always look a bit broken and distorted, because his interest is about the ambiance and the evoked feelings of a painting, and not about the exact and detailed representation of a reality. And the artist adds, not less important, is how some of his full coloured murals looked after a few months or years, how they lost almost all the brightness and saturation of the colours because of the strong sun in Spain. Depending on what the spot is asking for, according to the artist. The Spanish artist would like the spectators to see his work as a landscape, or a music composition, because it is not about meanings or direct messages of paintings. Wall paintings should talk with the spot, be in harmony with it or make a big contrast, we have to connect the elements and this is always enriching.“ For creating studio works, the artist defines himself as an irregular worker, with periods of tattooing and periods of painting.

Web research (verify): Emilio Cerezo, born 1986 Totana (Murcia), based Barcelona. Graffiti/street aliases 2001–2012; working under full name from 2012. Fine arts University of Granada 2004–2009; postgraduate drawing 2009–2010. Practice spans painting, murals, tattooing (UrbanPresents / KIFP summaries).

studio-giftig.location: Netherlands (duo: Niels van Swaemen, b. 1981 Eindhoven; Kaspar van Leek, b. 1983 Philipsburg, Sint Maarten)
studio-giftig.activeSince: Collaborating since 2007; dedicated to murals full-time from 2012 (studiogiftig.nl/about)
studio-giftig.heroHook: Dutch duo painting hyper-detailed (sur)realistic murals; they shoot their own reference so every image is autonomous. Name nods to a toxic-material paint storeroom.
studio-giftig.storyFullText:
Studio Giftig is the Netherlands-based duo of Niels van Swaemen (born 1981, Eindhoven) and Kaspar van Leek (born 1983, Philipsburg, Sint Maarten). They met in 2007, each already active on walls before joining forces.

They worked from a vacant warehouse that included a storage room for toxic substances and chemicals where they kept paint and spray cans; "Studio Giftig" takes its name from that room because giftig means toxic in Dutch.

At first one member studied at art school while the other worked as a graphic designer, so projects ran evenings and weekends; in 2012 they committed full time to murals and refined a shared, highly detailed (sur)realistic style.

A defining process claim on the same site is that all images are autonomously designed: they photograph their own objects and models to maintain total control of their visual language before painting.

Amsterdam Street Art and Inside Information appear on studiogiftig.nl as pull quotes; official CV pages stack international festivals and honors, including Street Art Cities “Best mural worldwide 2022” and Dutch Street Art Awards.

Awards stack through 2026—Society of Illustrators LA, Communication Arts shortlists, Applied Arts, American Illustration, and 3x3.

Exhibitions include Society of Illustrators annual shows in New York, Northern Contemporary in Toronto, Haight Street Art Center in San Francisco, and OCAD GradEx.

tiago-hesp.storyFullText:
Tiago Hesp opens his official profile like a manifesto—darkness first, then light—before the biography lands: Portuguese, born 1981, braiding mural art, music, illustration, scenography, and painting.

Lisbon theatre-school scenography meets graffiti since 1998, so stage space and wall share one grammar.

He says he paints to resolve himself, always from an aggressive black line chasing balance between shadow and color.

Solos stretch from early Lisbon rooms through 2025’s “É FOGO POSTO” in Porto, with Apaixonarte and municipal galleries in steady rotation.

Collectives and festivals count Underdogs, Mistaker Maker walls, STEP IN THE ARENA 2023, and Red Bull-era Lisbon landmarks.

twoone-hiroyasu-tsuri.location: Sant’Elia a Pianisi, Italy / Melbourne, Australia (hiroyasutsuri.com/about, 2021+)
twoone-hiroyasu-tsuri.activeSince: Born 1985 Yokohama; moved to Melbourne 2003; diploma Swinburne 2009; Berlin hub from 2014; Backwoods Gallery representation since 2010
twoone-hiroyasu-tsuri.heroHook: Yokohama-born TWOONE: Melbourne graffiti roots, "Raw Mark Making," museum-collected murals. CV spans Backwoods solos and giant civic walls in AU, JP, IT, CA.
twoone-hiroyasu-tsuri.storyFullText:
Hiroyasu Tsuri—TWOONE—was born in Yokohama and paints museum-scale murals collected by the National Gallery of Australia, Straat Museum, and MUCA Munich.

It recounts relocating to Melbourne in 2003 after high school, immersing in graffiti/street culture, coining "Raw Mark Making," and completing a Swinburne Diploma of Visual Arts & Multimedia in 2009.

Australia granted permanent residency in 2010 via a Distinguished Talent Visa the same year he joined Backwoods Gallery representation.

From 2014 Berlin served as a European hub for mural and performance collaborations; from 2021 he also bases studio practice in Sant’Elia a Pianisi, Italy.

His CV runs solos from 2008’s “Thousand Can Show” through 2025 Melbourne and Nagoya rooms, plus public murals across Australia, Japan, Italy, Canada, and beyond—Instagram anchors each wall.

tyler-shelton.activeSince: Turned Instagram @sheltonartco into a business at age 20; article context at age 29 (Dallas Observer)
tyler-shelton.heroHook: Texas pop painter mixing celebrity iconography with textured acrylic scraped by cards and screwdrivers—Dallas Observer detailed his Instagram growth and Thread Wallets collab.
tyler-shelton.storyFullText:
The Dallas Observer reports Tyler Shelton grew up with an art-teacher mother, committed to painting in high school, and formalized promotion through @sheltonartco from age 20, later breaking out financially around 2021.

Shelton is quoted saying painting lets him share what he thinks is cool without social awkwardness: "I can show you what I think is cool or how I feel or anything without the feeling of awkwardness…"

The same feature documents process quirks—preference for inexpensive matte acrylic, scraping paint with cards, occasional gravel or ash mixed in for texture, and long iPad sketch sessions before painting.

He discusses pivoting from classic-rock portraits to a more personal illustrative aesthetic influenced by acts like Mac DeMarco, arrived at through experimentation.

Austin Art Garage and Shelton Art Company storefronts list originals and prints, extending distribution beyond Instagram.
